Politicians in 2017 can learn from Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s legacy as both a progressive and conservative
qz.com
Quartz
4 min

Politicians in 2017 can learn from Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s legacy as both a progressive and conservative

Quartz · July 4, 2017

During the 2016 Republican presidential primary, The Washington Post\xe2\x80\x99s Bob Woodward and Robert Costa asked Donald Trump if he could be a \xe2\x80\x9cunifier\xe2\x80\x9d like Abraham Lincoln who expressed \xe2\x80\x9c\xe2\x80\x98Malice toward none, charity for all.\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d

Trump\xe2\x80\x99s answer was surprising, but the fact that he was asked the question is not. Lincoln has evolved into an icon of presidential leadership for both sides of the aisle.

In our book, \xe2\x80\x9cMemories of Lincoln and the Splintering of American Political Thought,\xe2\x80\x9d we studied reminiscences of Lincoln from the time of the president\xe2\x80\x99s death in 1865 through the passing of those who knew him personally in about 1900. We were particularly interested in understanding the rhetorical power of public messages that evoke the living Lincoln.

We believe how Lincoln is remembered by presidents and presidential candidates tells us much about their approach to governance. Trump\xe2\x80\x99s understanding of Lincoln sheds insight, we believe, into the controversies that swirl around the current president.

Lincoln memories as beacons

Memories of Abraham Lincoln have served as a \xe2\x80\x9cbeacon\xe2\x80\x9d for the nation since the hours after his assassination. The night the 16th president was shot, the streets of Washington, D.C. reportedly filled with angry Northerners contemplating revenge against any Southerner suspected in the assassination plot.

Years later, in recounting his memories of that frightful night, Sen. William M. Smith of Nevada credited Lincoln for quelling the storm. The spread of the simple epitaph\xe2\x80\x94 \xe2\x80\x9cWhat would Lincoln do?\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94helped quiet the crowd, Smith claimed, and revealed the force of Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s memory in beginning to heal a grieving nation.

Lincoln acquaintances like Smith created a cottage industry by publishing their first-person accounts of the Lincoln whom they talked with, walked with, witnessed and heard up close.

A generation removed

Subsequent generations used the recollections of the previous generation to keep Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s memory fresh\xe2\x80\x94and to repurpose his legacy.

U.S. presidents and presidential contenders have been among the most active interpreters of Lincoln memory as they attempt to score political points, bolster their credentials and unify the country during times of national turmoil.

Herbert Hoover turned Lincoln into a conservative; Franklin Roosevelt saw him as a progressive. Neither was writing fiction. Some memories of the historical Lincoln\xe2\x80\x94like his journey from a log cabin to the White House\xe2\x80\x94play up his commitment to \xe2\x80\x9cself-made\xe2\x80\x9d people and inspired entrepreneurial Hooverites. And Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s commitment to \xe2\x80\x9ccommon\xe2\x80\x9d people with simple tastes attracted New Dealers.

Candidates at the altar of Lincoln

Most presidential candidates genuflect at the altar of Lincoln when putting forth their presidential bona fides. In 2012, for example, Republican contender Mitt Romney recalled the exalted Lincoln when referring to him as \xe2\x80\x9c\xe2\x80\x98The last best hope of Earth.\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d

Democrat Barack Obama drew on memories of Lincoln as a mortal man during his reelection campaign against Romney: \xe2\x80\x9cI\xe2\x80\x99m far more mindful of my own failings, knowing exactly what Lincoln meant when he said, \xe2\x80\x98I have been driven to my knees many times by the overwhelming conviction that I had no place else to go.\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d

Trump used Lincoln to court black votes: \xe2\x80\x9cRepublicans are the Party of Lincoln\xe2\x80\xa6 Nothing means more to me than working to make our party the home of the African-American vote.\xe2\x80\x9d And in the same Washington Post interview, candidate Trump called Lincoln \xe2\x80\x9ca man\xe2\x80\xa6of great intelligence\xe2\x80\x9d who did \xe2\x80\x9ca very vital thing\xe2\x80\x9d for the country.

Seldom known for his humility, Trump admitted he wasn\xe2\x80\x99t likely to outdo Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s performance: \xe2\x80\x9cI can be more presidential than any president\xe2\x80\xa6except for Abraham Lincoln\xe2\x80\xa6 You can\xe2\x80\x99t out-top Abraham Lincoln.\xe2\x80\x9d That he took the oath of office with his hand on Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s Bible affirmed President Trump\xe2\x80\x99s desire to walk in Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s shadow.

Yet candidate Trump also revealed a lack of historical knowledge about Lincoln. He drew headlines for his repeated claim that few knew Lincoln was a Republican.

Trump also deviated from many contemporary presidents by hitching his wagon to Andrew Jackson without seeming to know much about him. Trump reported that Jackson was \xe2\x80\x9creally angry\xe2\x80\x9d by what \xe2\x80\x9che saw happening with\xe2\x80\xa6the Civil War\xe2\x80\x9d even though Jackson died 16 years before it began.

In celebrating Jackson\xe2\x80\x99s presidency, Trump offered a backhanded slap to Lincoln by suggesting that he failed to prevent the Civil War. Had \xe2\x80\x9cAndrew Jackson been a little later,\xe2\x80\x9d Trump suggested, \xe2\x80\x9cyou wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t have had the Civil War.\xe2\x80\x9d

Jackson, like Trump, left a legacy as the \xe2\x80\x9cpeople\xe2\x80\x99s tribune.\xe2\x80\x9d But he defended slavery and owned slaves, and Congress passed just one piece of legislation during his two terms\xe2\x80\x94the Indian Removal Act of 1830. That Trump displays a portrait of Jackson in the Oval Office rather than Lincoln shows whose memory guides his presidency.

Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s memory of unity

Perhaps most unprecedented is the way that Trump answered Woodward and Costa\xe2\x80\x99s question about unity. Although candidate Trump seemed certain he could \xe2\x80\x9cbring great unity\xe2\x80\x9d to the country, he seemed more fixated on his ability to provoke \xe2\x80\x9crage\xe2\x80\x9d in voters: \xe2\x80\x9cI do bring out rage\xe2\x80\xa6 I don\xe2\x80\x99t know if that\xe2\x80\x99s an asset or a liability.\xe2\x80\x9d

Trump gave more attention to the call for unity in his speech before a well-received Joint Session of Congress on Feb. 28, 2017: \xe2\x80\x9cI am here tonight to deliver a message of unity and strength, and it is a message deeply delivered from my heart.\xe2\x80\x9d But, his executive orders, Cabinet selections, policy positions, late-night and early-morning tweets have deepened divisions.

If Trump is serious about promoting national unity, he would do
well to ask himself the same question people did on those dark nights in D.C. after a president\xe2\x80\x99s assassination\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x9cWhat would Lincoln do?\xe2\x80\x9d He would learn that Lincoln was a serious student of history. He studied the Constitution and law closely. He surrounded himself with advisers of diverse perspectives. He prioritized humility over arrogance and empathy over revenge. As Trump considers delivering messages of unity to the country on its birthday, he would do well to heed the lessons\xe2\x80\x94and memories\xe2\x80\x94of Abraham Lincoln.

This article was originally published on The Conversation. Read the original article.

Quartz · July 4, 2017

d with, witnessed and heard up close. A generation removed Subsequent generations used the recollections of the previous generation to keep Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s memory fresh\xe2\x80\x94 and to repurpose his legacy. U.S. presidents and presidential contenders have been among the most active interpreters of Lincoln memory as they attempt to score political points, bolster their credentials and unify the country during times of national turmoil . Herbert Hoover turned Lincoln into a conservative; Franklin Roosevelt saw him as a progressive. Neither was writing fiction. Some memories of the historical Lincoln\xe2\x80\x94 like his journey from a log cabin to the White House\xe2\x80\x94 play up his commitment to \xe2\x80\x9cself- made\xe2\x80\x9d people and inspired entrepreneurial Hooverites. And Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s commitment to \xe2\x80\x9ccommon\xe2\x80\x9d people with simple tastes attracted New Dealers . Candidates at the altar of Lincoln Most presidential candidates genuflect at the altar of Lincoln when putting forth their presidential bona fides. In 2012, for example, Republican contender Mitt Romney recalled the exalted Lincoln when referring to him as \xe2\x80\x9c\xe2\x80\x98 The last best hope of Earth .\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d Democrat Barack Obama drew on memories of Lincoln as a mortal man during his reelection campaign against Romney: \xe2\x80\x9c I\xe2\x80\x99m far more mindful of my own failings , knowing exactly what Lincoln meant when he said, \xe2\x80\x98I have been driven to my knees many times by the overwhelming conviction that I had no place else to go.\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d Trump used Lincoln to court black votes: \xe2\x80\x9c Republicans are the Party of Lincoln \xe2\x80\xa6 Nothing means more to me than working to make our party the home of the African- American vote.\xe2\x80\x9d And in the same Washington Post interview , candidate Trump called Lincoln \xe2\x80\x9ca man\xe2\x80\xa6 of great intelligence\xe2\x80\x9d who did \xe2\x80\x9ca very vital thing\xe2\x80\x9d for the country. Seldom known for his humility, Trump admitted he wasn\xe2\x80\x99t likely to outdo Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s performance: \xe2\x80\x9cI can be more presidential than any president\xe2\x80\xa6 except for Abraham Lincoln\xe2\x80\xa6 You can\xe2\x80\x99t out- top Abraham Lincoln.\xe2\x80\x9d That he took the oath of office with his hand on Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s Bible affirmed President Trump\xe2\x80\x99s desire to walk in Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s shadow. Yet candidate Trump also revealed a lack of historical knowledge about Lincoln. He drew headlines for his repeated claim that few knew Lincoln was a Republican . Trump also deviated from many contemporary presidents by hitching his wagon to Andrew Jackson without seeming to know much about him. Trump reported that Jackson was \xe2\x80\x9creally angry\xe2\x80\x9d by what \xe2\x80\x9che saw happening with\xe2\x80\xa6 the Civil War\xe2\x80\x9d even though Jackson died 16 years before it began. In celebrating Jackson\xe2\x80\x99s presidency, Trump offered a backhanded slap to Lincoln by suggesting that he failed to prevent the Civil War. Had \xe2\x80\x9cAndrew Jackson been a little later,\xe2\x80\x9d Trump suggested, \xe2\x80\x9cyou wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t have had the Civil War .\xe2\x80\x9d Jackson, like Trump, left a legacy as the \xe2\x80\x9cpeople\xe2\x80\x99s tribune.\xe2\x80\x9d But he defended slavery and owned slaves, and Congress passed just one piece of legislation during his two terms\xe2\x80\x94 the Indian Removal Act of 1830. That Trump displays a portrait of Jackson in the Oval Office rather than Lincoln shows whose memory guides his presidency . Lincoln\xe2\x80\x99s memory of unity Perhaps most unprecedented is the way that Trump answered Woodward and Costa\xe2\x80\x99s question about unity . Although candidate Trump seemed certain he could \xe2\x80\x9cbring great unity\xe2\x80\x9d to the country, he seemed more fixated on his ability to provoke \xe2\x80\x9crage\xe2\x80\x9d in v
